one_line_takeaway: AgentSentry detects multi-turn indirect prompt injection by running boundary-anchored counterfactual dry-run re-executions to causally attribute an agent's next action to the user goal vs. untrusted tool/retrieval content, then purifies only the implicated context and lets the task continue -- reaching ASR=0% with 74.55% average Utility-under-Attack on AgentDojo, a 20.8-33.6 point UA improvement over the strongest prior baselines (Task Shield, MELON/MELON-Aug) at matched zero/near-zero ASR.
key_finding: On AgentDojo, AgentSentry achieves ASR = 0.00% across all three attack families and all three backbones while maintaining an average Utility-under-Attack (UA) of 74.55% and preserving Clean Utility (CU) with FPR = 0%. Concretely (Table 2, average over the three attack families): GPT-4o No-Defense UA 50.40%/ASR 26.17% vs AgentSentry UA 73.90%/ASR 0%; GPT-3.5-turbo No-Defense UA 40.18%/ASR 38.93% vs AgentSentry UA 70.16%/ASR 0%; Qwen3-Max No-Defense UA 57.73%/ASR 33.22% vs AgentSentry UA 79.58%/ASR 0%. Against the strongest prior baselines: vs Task Shield, AgentSentry improves average UA by +20.77 (GPT-4o, 53.13%->73.90%), +33.57 (GPT-3.5-turbo, 36.59%->70.16%), +29.27 (Qwen3-Max, 50.31%->79.58%) points while cutting ASR to 0; vs MELON-Aug, UA improves by +28.77/+32.41/+26.81 points across the three backbones with residual ASR (1.19%/2.14%/1.68%) eliminated; vs MELON, UA improves by +43.70/+42.38/+38.48 points. Other baselines showed instability: Tool Filter collapsed on Qwen3-Max (CU 6.19%, avg UA 9.44%) despite working reasonably on GPT-4o/GPT-3.5-turbo (avg UA 57.90%/60.11%); Repeat Prompt raised ASR to 95.24% on Qwen3-Max under Important Instructions even while boosting UA to 65.71%; the DeBERTa-v3 prompt-injection detector imposed large benign-utility loss (CU 41.24% on GPT-4o) while still leaving 14.29% ASR under Important Instructions on GPT-4o. Ablation on Workspace (560 tasks, Important Instructions, Qwen3-Max backbone) shows the full system reaches UA 90.36%/ASR 0.00%, and removing sanitized counterfactuals collapses performance to UA 58.21%/ASR 22.50%; replacing the windowed diagnostic with a single-step causal contrast gives UA 56.25%/ASR 21.79%; a fully heuristic (non-causal) detector baseline gives UA 56.07%/ASR 20.54% -- the paper attributes AgentSentry's gains specifically to mediator-side sanitized counterfactual causal attribution, not to surface heuristics or windowed aggregation alone (temporal aggregation disabled and tool-action-only ablations retain UA ~88-90% but still show non-zero ASR = 1.07%).
core_contribution: AgentSentry is an inference-time defense for tool-augmented LLM agents against indirect prompt injection (IPI). Its own contribution is: (1) modeling multi-turn IPI as a 'temporal causal takeover' -- a process by which, as untrusted tool/retrieval outputs accumulate in agent state, causal influence of the user goal attenuates while mediator (tool/retrieval)-driven influence dominates the agent's next-action decisions; (2) a temporal causal diagnostic protocol that performs controlled counterfactual re-executions at tool-return boundaries (four regimes: orig, mask, mask_sanitized, orig_sanitized, built by swapping the user input for a task-neutral probe x_mask and/or swapping the cached mediator content r_b for a sanitized/purified variant r_b^san), from which it computes plug-in Monte Carlo estimators of average causal effect (ACE), indirect effect (IE), and direct effect (DE) per boundary, plus windowed OLS trend slopes (beta_ACE, beta_IE) that feed a composite risk score R_b and a binary Takeover_b indicator; (3) causally gated context purification -- triggered only when diagnostics attribute the deviation to mediator-mediated causal dominance -- which suppresses instruction-carrying spans in the boundary context while preserving task-relevant evidence, followed by minimal action revision (Revise) rather than task termination, enabling 'safe continuation' of the original workflow. All diagnostics run in a dry-run mode (proposed tool calls are recorded but not executed) so probing does not itself trigger external side effects, which the paper frames as necessary for black-box, latency-bounded deployment.
threat_model: Black-box tool-augmented LLM agent accessed only via inference API (no model parameters, activations, or retraining access). The attacker cannot modify model parameters and does not need control over the user prompt. Instead the attacker has one or more of: (1) tool-result manipulation -- causing tools (email, documents, calendar, database records) to return attacker-controlled content; (2) retrieval manipulation -- biasing retrieved webpages/indexed documents to embed directives; (3) persistence via memory -- getting injected instructions stored as notes/state for delayed activation across turns. Attack objective: induce unauthorized high-impact actions (exfiltration, destructive operations) or goal drift that degrades compliance with user intent/policy. Success criterion: the agent commits a policy-violating external effect, or proposes a policy-violating high-impact operation absent intervention. Explicitly out of scope: compromise of the tool runtime itself (e.g., arbitrary code execution in the tool executor) and adversaries that directly tamper with the defender's own caching/replay mechanism. The defender is assumed able to restore execution to historical decision boundaries, perform dry-run re-executions, and replay cached or sanitized tool/retrieval responses.
limitations: Explicitly stated by the authors: (1) K=1 (single re-execution per regime, no bootstrap) is justified only because AgentDojo's injected directives manifest as short-horizon, boundary-adjacent control signals; the authors state this reflects benchmark characteristics rather than a fundamental property of the method, and that deployments with gradual/delayed takeovers would require larger K and bootstrap significance testing (higher inference cost, O(4K+B) additional agent invocations per boundary). (2) Dataset/discussion section explicitly notes that observed takeover timing in AgentDojo is dominated by short-horizon effects -- most injected objectives become actionable immediately after a contaminated tool read, with no sustained long-horizon progressive takeovers observed -- so the paper's own experiments do not exercise gradual multi-turn accumulation despite motivating the method with that scenario; they call for future benchmark suites modeling long-running agents and delayed IPI. (3) Explicitly out of scope: compromises of the tool runtime itself (arbitrary code execution in the tool executor) and adversaries who tamper with the defender's own caching/replay mechanism. (4) The counterfactual protocol incurs additional inference cost/latency versus undefended execution (multiple dry-run re-executions per boundary), acknowledged by the authors as a deployability tradeoff. (5) All evaluation is on a single benchmark family (AgentDojo) and three black-box LLM backbones; no evaluation against adaptive attackers aware of the counterfactual/purification mechanism is reported in the main text excerpt reviewed.

datasets_used: AgentDojo v0.1.35 benchmark, four task suites: Travel, Workspace (expanded from 6 to 14 injection tasks in v0.1.35), Banking, Slack; total 949 security test cases (up from 629 in earlier AgentDojo snapshots)
Three IPI attack families evaluated: Important Instructions, Tool Knowledge (both from AgentDojo), and InjecAgent
quantitative_results: Overall: AgentSentry ASR = 0.00% across all backbones/attack families; mean UA = 74.55% vs MELON-Aug mean UA 45.22%/ASR 1.67%
GPT-4o average over 3 attack families: No Defense UA 50.40%/ASR 26.17%; AgentSentry UA 73.90%/ASR 0.00%; Task Shield UA 53.13%/ASR 4.93%
GPT-3.5-turbo average: No Defense UA 40.18%/ASR 38.93%; AgentSentry UA 70.16%/ASR 0.00%; Task Shield UA 36.59%/ASR 6.65%
Qwen3-Max average: No Defense UA 57.73%/ASR 33.22%; AgentSentry UA 79.58%/ASR 0.00%; Task Shield UA 50.31%/ASR 5.66%
Clean Utility preserved: GPT-4o CU 78.35% (matches No-Defense CU), GPT-3.5-turbo CU 77.32%, Qwen3-Max CU 83.51%, all with FPR = 0.00%

Ablation on Workspace/Important Instructions/Qwen3-Max (560 tasks): Full AgentSentry UA 90.36%/ASR 0.00%; sanitized counterfactuals removed UA 58.21%/ASR 22.50%; single-step causal contrast UA 56.25%/ASR 21.79%; weak masking (paraphrasing) UA 56.07%/ASR 23.57%; weak masking (tool-instruction deletion) UA 56.43%/ASR 22.86%; tool-action-only decision signal UA 88.21%/ASR 1.07%; temporal aggregation disabled UA 88.57%/ASR 1.07%; heuristic detection without causal re-execution UA 56.07%/ASR 20.54%
Qwen3-Max Tool Filter instability: CU collapses to 6.19%, average UA to 9.44% (vs. GPT-4o CU 72.16%/avg UA 57.90% and GPT-3.5-turbo CU 73.20%/avg UA 60.11% for the same defense)
Repeat Prompt on Qwen3-Max under Important Instructions: UA 65.71% but ASR rises to 95.24%
baselines_compared: Delimiting (prompt-level, isolates external content with textual markers)
Repeat Prompt / prompt sandwiching (restates user objective before/after context)
Tool Filter (AgentDojo's built-in allowlist over tool invocations)
DeBERTa Detector (DeBERTa-v3 prompt-injection classifier applied to tool-return content)
Task Shield (policy-aware alignment guard checking action-to-goal justification)
